does: This paper challenges the assumption that safety properties of individual large language models automatically compose into safe multi-agent systems, arguing instead that interaction topology fundamentally determines safety outcomes in agentic AI. Through empirical analysis across multiple model families and scales, the authors identify three persistent failure modes: ordering instability, where system behavior depends on agent sequencing; information cascades, where initial decisions propagate regardless of correctness; and functional collapse, where systems optimize for fairness metrics while losing meaningful risk discrimination. The core contribution is demonstrating that these topology-driven pathologies persist and actually strengthen with larger, more capable models, contradicting the intuition that scaling alone improves safety in multi-agent scenarios.
connects: This work extends research on multi-agent AI systems and emergent behavior in large language models by focusing specifically on interaction structure rather than individual model capabilities. It builds on existing concerns about LLM deployment in high-stakes settings and relates to work on alignment that typically assumes safety can be achieved through training individual agents. The paper connects to research on cascade effects and information flow in collective decision-making, as well as prior work examining how model composition fails in ways invisible to standard benchmarks. It responds to and critiques the dominant paradigm in AI safety that equates model capabilities with system-level safety guarantees.
enables: This position establishes interaction topology as a critical design variable for safe agentic AI systems, opening new research directions into optimal communication structures, information aggregation mechanisms, and deliberation protocols that actively prevent cascades and order-dependence. The findings suggest future work should focus on developing topology-aware safety verification methods and designing agent interaction patterns that are robust to harmful information flow structures. This work enables investigation of how different aggregation schemes, feedback mechanisms, and hierarchical arrangements affect safety properties, and points toward the need for safety architectures that consider information topology as fundamentally as we consider model training. It also suggests that deployment strategies and institutional arrangements around multi-agent systems may matter as much as the underlying model capabilities.
